name: lerpAngle
signature: (a: number, b: number, t: number) => number
description:
Interpolate between two angles along the shortest arc (no spinning the long way around).

The angle-aware cousin of {@link lerp } — use it to smoothly rotate something
toward a target heading without sudden flips at the 0/360° boundary.

params.name: a
params.text: Start angle in radians, returned when `t` is 0.

params.name: b
params.text: Target angle in radians, returned when `t` is 1.

returns: The interpolated angle in radians.

name: shortestAngleBetween
description:
The smallest signed angle to rotate from `a` to `b`, taking the short way around.

Solves the classic "360° seam" problem: rotating from 350° to 10° should be
`+20°`, not `-340°`. Positive results turn counter-clockwise.
returns: The shortest signed delta in `(-π, π]`.

name: wrapAngle
description:
Normalize an angle to the range `(-π, π]`.

Collapses any angle (including ones that have wound past a full turn) to its
canonical equivalent, so `3π` and `π` are treated as the same direction.

params.name: radians
params.text: The angle to normalize, in radians.

returns: The equivalent angle in `(-π, π]`.

name: criticalDamping
description:
The damping coefficient that makes {@link springValue} *critically* damped — the
fastest approach to the target with no overshoot — for a given `stiffness` and `mass`.

Critical damping is `c = 2·√(k·m)`. Pass less to {@link springValue}'s `damping` for
bounce (underdamped), more for a sluggish crawl (overdamped). With the default
stiffness of 170 this is `≈ 26`, which is why 26 is the default damping.

name: delay
signature: (elapsedMs: number, delayMs: number, durationMs: number) => number
description: Delay a progress value by `delayMs`, returning 0 until the delay has elapsed.

name: loop
description: Map elapsed time into a repeating 0..1 progress value.

name: springValue
signature: (state: SpringState, target: number, { stiffness, damping, mass, deltaSeconds, }?: SpringOptions) => SpringState
description: Advance a damped spring one fixed simulation step.
example:
let s = { value: 0, velocity: 0 };
s = springValue(s, 100); // => state nudged toward 100; call each frame

name: stagger
signature: (index: number, elapsedMs: number, durationMs: number, staggerMs: number) => number
description: Return the delayed progress for an item in a staggered list.

name: ticker
description: Run a callback every animation frame and return a small cancellation handle.
example:
const handle = ticker(({ elapsed }) => draw(elapsed));
// later: handle.cancel();

name: tweenFrame
signature: <T extends Record<string, number>>(spec: TweenObjectSpec<T>, elapsedMs: number, durationMs: number, easingOrOptions?: EasingFunction | TweenValueOptions) => T
description: Alias for tweenObject when you want to sample animation state one frame at a time.

name: tweenObject
signature: <T extends Record<string, number>>(spec: TweenObjectSpec<T>, elapsedMs: number, durationMs: number, easingOrOptions?: EasingFunction | TweenValueOptions) => T
description: Return every numeric property of an object tween at a specific elapsed time.

name: tweenValue
signature: (from: number, to: number, elapsedMs: number, durationMs: number, easingOrOptions?: EasingFunction | TweenValueOptions) => number
description: Return the value of a scalar tween at a specific elapsed time.

name: yoyo
description: Map elapsed time into a reversing 0..1..0 progress value.

name: ballBounce
signature: (ball: Ball, stage: Container, restitution?: number) => void
description:
Advance a ball one frame under gravity, bouncing it off the container walls.

A simple Euler-integration physics step: applies gravity to vertical velocity,
moves the ball, then reflects and damps its velocity (restitution + friction)
whenever it hits a wall. **Mutates `ball`** in place.

params.name: ball
params.text: The ball to advance (its `x`, `y`, `vx`, `vy` are mutated).

params.name: stage
params.text: The bounding container (`width`/`height`) the ball bounces inside.

params.name: restitution
params.text:
Bounciness: fraction of speed kept on each bounce (0–1).
`1` is a perfectly elastic, never-tiring bounce; lower values lose energy faster.
Defaults to `0.72`.

returns: Nothing — `ball` is updated in place.
example:
ballBounce(ball, { x: 0, y: 0, width: 800, height: 600 });      // default bounce
ballBounce(ball, stage, 0.95);                                   // extra bouncy

name: ballToBallBounce
signature: (ball1: Ball, ball2: Ball, spring?: number) => void
description:
Push two overlapping balls apart with a spring-like force (soft collision response).

When the balls overlap, applies an equal-and-opposite force proportional to the
overlap, easing them apart rather than resolving the collision instantly.
**Mutates both balls'** velocities in place.

params.name: spring
params.text: Stiffness of the separating force, 0–1. Defaults to `0.05`.

returns: Nothing — both balls' velocities are updated in place.
example: ballToBallBounce(a, b); // call for each pair, once per frame

name: bezierPoint
signature: (p0: Point, p1: Point, p2: Point, p3: Point, t: number) => Point
description:
Find the point at parameter `t` along a cubic Bézier curve.

Implemented via de Casteljau's algorithm — repeated linear interpolation between
the control points, which is the geometric definition of a Bézier curve.
returns: The `{ x, y }` point on the curve at `t`.

name: Boid
description: One agent in the flock: where it is and where it is heading.

name: BoidsOptions
description: Tunable weights and radii for {@link boidsStep}.

name: boidsStep
signature: (boids: Boid[], options?: Partial<BoidsOptions>, bounds?: FlockBounds) => void
description:
Advance a flock by one tick, in place, using Craig Reynolds' three boids rules.

Accelerations are computed for every boid first (reading the *old* state), then
applied — so the result is independent of array order and fully deterministic for
the same inputs (no RNG, no clock). Neighbor search is the naive O(n²) all-pairs
scan: clear and correct, but for thousands of boids swap in a spatial grid.

params.name: boids
params.text: The flock; each boid's `position` and `velocity` are mutated.

params.name: bounds
params.text: Optional toroidal wrap so the flock never flies off-screen.

returns: Nothing; `boids` is updated in place.

name: DEFAULT_BOIDS_OPTIONS
description: Sensible starting values; spread-override any field you like.

name: Flock
description:
An instantiable flock that owns its agents and advances them with {@link boidsStep}.

Pure of any framework — it never touches a canvas, the DOM, or the clock — so it
runs identically in a browser, in Node, or in a test. Build one, call `step()` once
per frame (optionally passing a wandering target), then read `boids` to draw them.

name: FlockBounds
description: Toroidal bounds — boids that leave one edge re-enter the opposite one.

name: centerOnParent
description: Compute the top-left position that centers one box inside another.

params.name: item
params.text: The child box being centered (its `width`/`height` are used).

params.name: parent
params.text: The container box to center within.

returns: The `{ x, y }` top-left coordinate that centers `item` in `parent`.
example: centerOnParent({ width: 20, height: 10 }, { width: 100, height: 50 }); // => { x: 40, y: 20 }

name: circleFromThreePoints
signature: (point1: Point, point2: Point, point3: Point) => { radius: number; center: Point; }
description: Find the unique circle (the circumcircle) passing through three points.
returns: The circle's `radius` and `center` `{ x, y }`. (Degenerate for collinear points.)
example:
circleFromThreePoints({ x: 5, y: 0 }, { x: 0, y: 5 }, { x: -5, y: 0 });
// => { radius: 5, center: { x: 0, y: 0 } }

name: circleToCircle
signature: (x1: number, y1: number, r1: number, x2: number, y2: number, r2: number) => boolean
description: Test whether two circles overlap (or touch).
returns: `true` if the distance between centers is `<= r1 + r2`.

name: circleToRect
signature: (cx: number, cy: number, cr: number, rx: number, ry: number, rw: number, rh: number) => boolean
description:
Test whether a circle overlaps an axis-aligned rectangle.

Works by clamping the circle's center to the rectangle to find the closest
point on the rect, then checking if that point is within the radius.
returns: `true` if the circle and rectangle overlap or touch.

name: clamp
signature: (value: number, min: number, max: number) => number
description: Constrain a value to a range, so it never goes below `min` or above `max`.
returns: `value` limited to the range `[min, max]`.

name: circleCircle
description: Test whether two circles overlap (object-argument form of `circleToCircle`).
returns: `true` if the circles touch or overlap, including exact tangency.
example: circleCircle({ x: 0, y: 0, radius: 5 }, { x: 6, y: 0, radius: 5 }); // => true

name: lineCircle
description:
Test whether a line segment intersects a circle (object-argument form of `lineToCircle`).

Projects the circle's center onto the segment, clamps that projection to the
segment bounds, and checks whether the closest point lies within the radius.
This also handles degenerate zero-length segments safely.
returns: `true` if the segment touches or crosses the circle.
example: lineCircle({ startPoint: { x: 0, y: 0 }, endPoint: { x: 10, y: 0 } }, { x: 5, y: 0, radius: 2 }); // => true

name: lineLine
signature: (line1: Line, line2: Line) => { readonly hit: false; intersectionX?: undefined; intersectionY?: undefined; } | { hit: boolean; intersectionX: number; intersectionY: number; } | { hit: boolean; intersectionX?: undefined; intersectionY?: undefined; }
description:
Test whether two line segments cross, and report where (object-argument form).

Unlike the boolean `lineToLine`, this returns the intersection coordinates when
the segments meet — useful for drawing a marker at the crossing.

Parallel or collinear segments return `{ hit: false }` in this implementation.
returns: `{ hit: true, intersectionX, intersectionY }` if they cross, else `{ hit: false }`.
example:
const r = lineLine(a, b);
if (r.hit) drawDot(r.intersectionX, r.intersectionY);

name: linePoint
description:
Test whether a point lies on a line segment (object-argument form).

Projects the point onto the segment, clamps that projection to the segment
bounds, and checks whether the nearest point lands within a built-in `0.1`
unit tolerance.
returns: `true` if the point sits on the segment within the built-in `0.1` tolerance.
example: linePoint({ startPoint: { x: 0, y: 0 }, endPoint: { x: 10, y: 0 } }, { x: 5, y: 0 }); // => true

name: pointCircle
description: Test whether a point falls inside or on a circle (object-argument form of `pointToCircle`).
returns: `true` if the point is within `radius` of the circle center.
example: pointCircle({ x: 1, y: 1 }, { x: 0, y: 0, radius: 5 }); // => true

name: polygonCircle
description:
Test whether a circle overlaps a polygon.

Walks each polygon edge and tests it against the circle. If no edge touches
the circle, it then checks whether the circle's center sits inside the polygon,
which catches the fully-contained case.
returns: `true` if the circle touches an edge or sits inside the polygon.
example: polygonCircle({ vertices: square }, { x: 5, y: 5, radius: 2 }); // => true

name: polygonLine
description:
Test whether a line segment crosses any edge of a polygon.

Walks each polygon edge and runs a segment-vs-segment intersection test
against `line`.

This checks edge crossings only. A line segment fully contained inside the
polygon, with no boundary intersection, returns `false`.
returns: `true` if the line crosses a polygon edge.
example: polygonLine({ vertices: square }, { startPoint: { x: -5, y: 5 }, endPoint: { x: 15, y: 5 } }); // => true

name: polygonPoint
description:
Test whether a point is inside a polygon (ray-casting / even-odd rule).

Casts a horizontal ray from the point and counts how many polygon edges it
crosses — an odd count means the point is inside. Works for both convex and
concave polygons.

Boundary points are not special-cased. A point on an edge or vertex follows
the raw parity result from the ray test, so edge cases may differ depending
on which boundary segment is hit.
returns: `true` if the point is inside the polygon.
example: polygonPoint({ vertices: square }, { x: 5, y: 5 }); // => true

name: polygonPolygon
description:
Test whether two polygons overlap (object-argument form).

Walks each edge of the first polygon and tests it against the second. If no
edges cross, it then checks both containment directions by testing a sample
vertex from each polygon against the other polygon.
returns: `true` if the polygons intersect or one contains the other.
example: polygonPolygon({ vertices: squareA }, { vertices: squareB }); // => true when overlapping

name: colorFamily
description:
Build an ordered palette of colors that all belong to one named hue family.

The spiritual successor to a "give me a random blue" helper: instead of one
random color, you get an evenly-spread *range* within the family (all blues,
all greens, …). Hue marches across the family's band while lightness arcs up
toward the middle of the ramp, so the set reads as a coherent, lively palette
rather than a flat gradient. Deterministic — same args, same palette.
returns: An array of {@link RGB} colors, ordered across the family's hue range.
example:
colorFamily("blue", 5);   // five blues, light→deep→light across the band
colorFamily("all", 12);   // a full evenly-spaced rainbow

name: HSL
description: An HSL color: hue 0–360°, saturation and lightness 0–100%.

name: hslToRgb
description:
Convert an HSL color back to RGB.

Inverse of {@link rgbToHsl}. Hue wraps, so values outside 0–360 are accepted.
returns: Channels rounded to integers 0–255.

name: HUE_FAMILIES
description:
Hue ranges (degrees) for each named family. `red` uses a negative start so it
straddles the 0°/360° seam cleanly — {@link hslToRgb} wraps the hue for you.

name: HueFamily
description: Named hue families and the wheel angle range (in degrees) each spans.

name: lerpColor
signature: (a: RGB, b: RGB, t: number) => RGB
description:
Interpolate two colors in RGB space — a straight per-channel {@link lerp}.

This is the obvious approach, and it has a famous pitfall: blending two vivid
complementary colors (e.g. blue↔yellow) passes through a muddy gray at the
midpoint, because the midpoint of opposite channels is ~middle-gray. Compare
with {@link lerpColorHsl}.
returns: The blended color (channels may be fractional).

name: lerpColorHsl
signature: (a: RGB, b: RGB, t: number) => RGB
description:
Interpolate two colors through HSL space, rotating hue the short way around.

Because hue, saturation, and lightness move independently, the blend stays
vivid instead of collapsing to gray — the colors travel *around* the wheel
rather than *through* its desaturated center. This is the perceptually nicer
default for gradients. Contrast with {@link lerpColor}.
returns: The blended color, channels rounded to 0–255.
example: lerpColorHsl({ r: 0, g: 0, b: 255 }, { r: 255, g: 255, b: 0 }, 0.5); // a vivid mid-hue, not gray

name: RGB
description: An RGB color. Channels are 0–255 (may be fractional mid-interpolation).

name: rgbToCss
description: Format an RGB color as a CSS `rgb()` string, rounding channels to integers.

name: rgbToHsl
description:
Convert an RGB color to HSL.

Hue is the angle on the color wheel; saturation is how vivid (vs. gray) the
color is; lightness is how close to black/white. Separating these is what lets
you interpolate colors without dragging them through gray — see {@link lerpColorHsl}.

name: deCasteljau
signature: (points: Point[], t: number) => { point: Point; levels: Point[][]; }
description:
Evaluate any-degree Bézier curve at parameter `t` using de Casteljau's algorithm.

De Casteljau works by repeatedly lerping between adjacent control points until
a single point remains. At each level of reduction you get the intermediate
"construction" points that make the algorithm visual — pass the returned `levels`
array to your canvas code to draw the animated hull the way a textbook would.
Works for quadratic (3 pts), cubic (4 pts), quartic (5 pts), or any degree.

params.name: points
params.text: The ordered control points defining the curve (any length >= 2).

params.name: t
params.text: Position along the curve, 0–1 (0 = start, 1 = end).

returns:
An object `{ point, levels }` where `point` is the `{ x, y }` on the
curve at `t`, and `levels` is an array of point arrays — `levels[0]` is the
original control polygon, each subsequent level is the next reduction step,
and the last level contains only `point`.

name: degToRad
description: Convert an angle from degrees to radians.
returns: The equivalent angle in radians.

name: dft
signature: (pts: Point[]) => { freq: number; amp: number; phase: number; }[]
description:
Discrete Fourier Transform of a list of 2D points (treated as complex numbers).

Decomposes a path into a sum of rotating circles (epicycles) — the math behind
"draw anything with spinning vectors" animations. Each output entry is one
circle: its frequency, radius (`amp`), and starting angle (`phase`). Results are
sorted by amplitude so the most significant circles come first.

params.name: pts
params.text: The input path as an array of points (`x` = real, `y` = imaginary).

returns: One `{ freq, amp, phase }` per input sample, sorted by descending `amp`.
example: const circles = dft(pathPoints); // feed into an epicycle drawer

name: distance
description: Euclidean (straight-line) distance between two points.
returns: The distance between `p1` and `p2`.

name: distributeAroundCircle
signature: (circleCenter: Point, radius: number, totalItems: number) => Point[]
description: Evenly space N points around a circle (e.g. for a radial menu or clock face).

params.name: totalItems
params.text: How many points to distribute. The first sits at angle 0 (3 o'clock).

returns: An array of `totalItems` points spread evenly around the circle.
example: distributeAroundCircle({ x: 0, y: 0 }, 10, 4); // => 4 points at 3, 6, 9, 12 o'clock

name: easeIn
description: Quadratic ease-in — starts slow, accelerates toward the end.
returns: The eased value (`t²`), 0 at `t=0` and 1 at `t=1`.

name: easeInCubic
description: Cubic ease-in.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInOut
description: Quadratic ease-in-out — accelerates then decelerates, symmetric about `t=0.5`.
returns: The eased value, 0 at `t=0` and 1 at `t=1`.

name: easeInOutCubic
description: Cubic ease-in-out.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInOutQuad
description: Quadratic ease-in-out. Legacy alias from the vanilla JS utilities.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInOutQuart
description: Quartic ease-in-out.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInOutQuint
description: Quintic ease-in-out.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInQuad
description: Quadratic ease-in. Legacy alias from the vanilla JS utilities.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInQuart
description: Quartic ease-in.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeInQuint
description: Quintic ease-in.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeOut
description: Quadratic ease-out — starts fast, decelerates toward the end.
returns: The eased value, 0 at `t=0` and 1 at `t=1`.

name: easeOutBounce
description: Bounce ease-out using Robert Penner's common piecewise curve.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeOutCubic
description: Cubic ease-out.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeOutElastic
description: Elastic ease-out with a short overshoot.
returns: The eased value.
example: easeOutElastic(1); // => 1 (settles on the target after overshooting)

name: easeOutQuad
description: Quadratic ease-out. Legacy alias from the vanilla JS utilities.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeOutQuart
description: Quartic ease-out.
returns: The eased value.

name: easeOutQuint
description: Quintic ease-out.
returns: The eased value.

name: linear
description: Linear easing — constant speed; the identity curve.
returns: The eased value, equal to `t`.

name: equilateralTriangle
signature: (radius: number, centerPoint: Point, angle: number) => { point1: Point; point2: Point; point3: Point; }
description: Compute the three vertices of an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle.

params.name: radius
params.text: Distance from the center to each vertex (the circumradius).

params.name: angle
params.text: Rotation in radians; `point1` sits at this angle, the others 120° apart.

returns: The three corners as `{ point1, point2, point3 }`.
example: equilateralTriangle(5, { x: 0, y: 0 }, 0); // point1 at { x: 5, y: 0 }

name: findPointAroundCircle
signature: (circleCenter: Point, radius: number, percentageAroundCircle: number) => Point
description: Find the point a given percentage of the way around a circle.

params.name: percentageAroundCircle
params.text: Position around the circle, `0`–`100`. 0 is at 3 o'clock; 25 is at 6 o'clock.

returns: The `{ x, y }` point on the circle at that percentage.

name: gameOfLifeStep
signature: (grid: ArrayLike<number>, cols: number, rows: number) => Uint8Array
description:
Compute the next generation of Conway's Game of Life.

Applies the classic rules to every cell — a live cell survives with 2 or 3 live
neighbours, a dead cell is born with exactly 3 — using a toroidal (wrap-around)
grid so the edges connect. Returns a new grid rather than mutating the input.

params.name: grid
params.text: The current generation as a flat row-major array of `0`/`1` (length `cols * rows`).

returns: A new `Uint8Array` holding the next generation.
example: grid = gameOfLifeStep(grid, cols, rows); // advance one tick

name: getPointOnLine
signature: (p1: Point, p2: Point, t: number) => Point
description: Find a point partway along the straight line between two points (lerp in 2D).

params.name: t
params.text: Fraction along the line; `0.5` is the midpoint.

returns: The interpolated `{ x, y }` point.

name: getRandomColors
signature: (str?: string) => { H: number; S: number; L: number; }
description:
Pick a random HSL color, optionally biased toward a named hue family.

Returns hue/saturation/lightness components (not a CSS string) so the caller can
format them. Picking the hue from a restricted range keeps random colors within
a recognizable family (e.g. all blues).

params.name: str
params.text:
A color family to stay within: `"red"`, `"orange"`, `"yellow"`,
`"green"`, or `"blue"`. Anything else (default `"all"`) spans the full wheel.

returns: `{ H, S, L }` — hue (0–360), saturation (50–100), lightness (25–75).
example:
const { H, S, L } = getRandomColors("blue");
const css = `hsl(${H}, ${S}%, ${L}%)`;

name: getRotation
description:
Get the angle (in radians) pointing from one point toward another.

Handy for rotating a sprite so it "faces" a target. Uses `Math.atan2`, so the
result is in the range `-π`…`π`, measured clockwise from the positive x-axis.
returns: The heading angle in radians.
example: getRotation({ x: 0, y: 0 }, { x: 0, y: 1 }); // => Math.PI / 2

name: getTriangleData
signature: (p1: Point, p2: Point) => { dx: number; dy: number; distance: number; }
description: Get the horizontal/vertical deltas and distance between two points.
returns: `{ dx, dy, distance }` — the run, the rise, and the hypotenuse.
example: getTriangleData({ x: 0, y: 0 }, { x: 3, y: 4 }); // => { dx: 3, dy: 4, distance: 5 }

name: triangleDataFromLine
signature: (startPoint: Point, endPoint: Point) => { angleInDegrees: number; remainingAngle: number; hypotenuse: number; adjacent: number; opposite: number; }
description:
Solve the right triangle formed by a line and its horizontal/vertical legs.

Treats the line as the hypotenuse and derives the side lengths and angles via
basic SOH-CAH-TOA trigonometry.

params.name: endPoint
params.text: The other end of the line.

returns: `{ angleInDegrees, remainingAngle, hypotenuse, adjacent, opposite }`.
example:
triangleDataFromLine({ x: 0, y: 0 }, { x: 4, y: 3 });
// => { angleInDegrees: 36, remainingAngle: 54, hypotenuse: 5, adjacent: 4, opposite: 3 }

name: grStep
signature: (orbiter: { x: number; y: number; vx: number; vy: number; }, sun: { x: number; y: number; mass: number; }, grStrength: number) => void
description:
Advance an orbiting body one frame under gravity with a general-relativity correction.

Like Newtonian gravity but with an extra `1 + grStrength/r²` term that strengthens
the pull at close range — this is what makes orbits *precess* (the famous anomaly
in Mercury's orbit). **Mutates `orbiter`** in place.

params.name: grStrength
params.text: Strength of the relativistic correction; `0` reduces to Newtonian gravity.

returns: Nothing — `orbiter` is updated in place.
example: grStep(planet, sun, 800); // call once per frame to see the orbit precess

name: inverseLerp
signature: (a: number, b: number, value: number) => number
description:
The inverse of {@link lerp }: find the fraction `t` that produces `value` between `a` and `b`.

Answers "how far along am I?" — e.g. turning a current value into a 0–1 progress.

params.name: value
params.text: The value to locate within the range.

returns: The fraction `t` such that `lerp(a, b, t) === value`. Returns `0` if `a === b`.

name: lensDeflection
signature: (x: number, y0: number, lx: number, ly: number, mass: number) => number
description:
Approximate how far a light ray is bent as it passes a massive object (gravitational lensing).

A simplified model of Einstein's light-bending: the deflection grows with the
lens `mass` and falls off with distance from it (the impact parameter `b`),
which is why objects behind a galaxy appear warped into arcs.

params.name: mass
params.text: Mass of the lensing object; larger bends light more.

returns: The vertical deflection to apply at this `x` (0 when far from the lens).
example: const dy = lensDeflection(rayX, rayY, lensX, lensY, 2000);

name: lerp
signature: (a: number, b: number, t: number) => number
description: Linearly interpolate between two numbers.

params.name: t
params.text: Interpolation factor. 0–1 stays within the range; values outside extrapolate.

returns: The interpolated value `a + (b - a) * t`.

name: lineLength
description: Get the length of a line segment.
returns: The straight-line distance between the two endpoints.
example: lineLength({ startPoint: { x: 0, y: 0 }, endPoint: { x: 3, y: 4 } }); // => 5

name: lineToCircle
signature: (x1: number, y1: number, x2: number, y2: number, cx: number, cy: number, cr: number) => boolean
description:
Test whether a line segment intersects a circle.

Finds the closest point on the segment to the circle's center and checks
whether it lies within the radius. This also handles degenerate zero-length
segments safely.
returns: `true` if the segment touches or crosses the circle.

name: lineToLine
signature: (x1: number, y1: number, x2: number, y2: number, x3: number, y3: number, x4: number, y4: number) => boolean
description:
Test whether two line segments cross.

Parallel or collinear segments return `false` in this implementation.
returns: `true` if the two segments intersect (parallel segments return `false`).

name: lineToPoint
signature: (x1: number, y1: number, x2: number, y2: number, px: number, py: number, threshold: number) => boolean
description: Test whether a point lies on a line segment, within a tolerance.

params.name: threshold
params.text: How close (in units) the point must be to count as "on the line".

returns: `true` if the point is within `threshold` of the segment.

name: lineToRect
signature: (x1: number, y1: number, x2: number, y2: number, rx: number, ry: number, rw: number, rh: number) => boolean
description:
Test whether a line segment crosses any edge of an axis-aligned rectangle.

Note: this checks the four edges only — a segment lying entirely *inside* the
rectangle is not reported as a hit.
returns: `true` if the segment crosses a rectangle edge.

name: mapRange
signature: (value: number, inMin: number, inMax: number, outMin: number, outMax: number) => number
description:
Re-map a number from one range into another (also known as "remap").

Linear and unclamped: a value outside the input range maps proportionally
outside the output range. Pair with {@link clamp } if you need it bounded.
returns: `value` rescaled from the input range to the output range.

name: moveAlongLine
signature: (origin: Point, destination: Point, ratio: number) => Point
description: Find the point a given fraction of the way from one point to another.

params.name: ratio
params.text: Fraction of the way along; `0.5` is the midpoint.

returns: The interpolated `{ x, y }` point.

name: moveToward
signature: (obj: Point, dest: Point, speed: number) => number
description:
Step a moving object toward a destination at a fixed speed, snapping on arrival.

**Mutates `obj`** — its `x`/`y` are advanced by up to `speed` units toward `dest`.
If it's within one step, it lands exactly on `dest`.

params.name: obj
params.text: The point to move (mutated in place).

params.name: speed
params.text: Maximum distance to travel this call.

returns: The heading angle (radians) from `obj` toward `dest`, for orienting the sprite.
example:
const ball = { x: 0, y: 0 };
moveToward(ball, { x: 10, y: 0 }, 2); // ball is now { x: 2, y: 0 }; returns 0

name: numberWithCommas
description: Format a number with comma thousands-separators.
returns: The number as a string with a comma every three digits.

name: gravitationalStep
signature: (orbiter: { x: number; y: number; vx: number; vy: number; }, body: { x: number; y: number; mass: number; }) => void
description:
Advance an orbiting body one frame under Newtonian gravity (inverse-square attraction).

Each step pulls the orbiter toward the central body with a force proportional to
`mass / r²`, then integrates velocity and position — enough to produce stable
orbits. **Mutates `orbiter`** in place.
returns: Nothing — `orbiter` is updated in place.
example: gravitationalStep(planet, sun); // call once per frame

name: pingPong
description:
Bounce a value back and forth between 0 and `length` as `t` increases.

Produces a triangle wave: `0 → length → 0 → length …`. Perfect for seamless
looping motion (a sprite sliding to the edge and back without a visible jump).

params.name: t
params.text: The input value, usually an ever-increasing time or counter.

params.name: length
params.text: The peak of the bounce; output ranges over `[0, length]`.

returns: The ping-ponged value in `[0, length]`. Returns `0` if `length <= 0`.
example:
pingPong(15, 10); // => 5  (past the peak, heading back down)
pingPong(20, 10); // => 0  (full cycle, back to start)

name: pointToCircle
signature: (px: number, py: number, cx: number, cy: number, radius: number) => boolean
description: Test whether a point falls inside (or on the edge of) a circle.
returns: `true` if the point is within `radius` of the center.

name: pointToPolygon
signature: (px: number, py: number, vertices: Point[]) => boolean
description:
Test whether a point falls inside a polygon (ray-casting / even-odd rule).

Casts a horizontal ray from the point and counts how many polygon edges it
crosses — an odd count means the point is inside. Works for both convex and
concave polygons.
returns: `true` if the point lies inside the polygon.

name: pointToRect
signature: (px: number, py: number, rx: number, ry: number, rw: number, rh: number) => boolean
description: Test whether a point falls inside (or on the edge of) an axis-aligned rectangle.
returns: `true` if the point lies within the rectangle bounds, including edges.

name: polygonToPolygon
description:
Test whether two polygons overlap, by checking if either has a vertex inside the other.

Uses ray-casting (the point-in-polygon test) on each polygon's vertices. This
catches most overlaps but can miss edge-only crossings where no vertex is contained.

params.name: poly1
params.text: First polygon as an array of points.

params.name: poly2
params.text: Second polygon as an array of points.

returns: `true` if a vertex of either polygon lies inside the other.
example: polygonToPolygon(squareA, squareB); // => true when they overlap

name: quadraticBezier
signature: (t: number, p0: Point, p1: Point, p2: Point) => Point
description:
Find the point at parameter `t` along a quadratic Bézier curve (one control point).

Uses the closed-form quadratic Bézier formula
`(1-t)²·p0 + 2(1-t)t·p1 + t²·p2`.

params.name: p1
params.text: The single control handle that bends the curve.

returns: The `{ x, y }` point on the curve at `t`.

name: radToDeg
description: Convert an angle from radians to degrees.
returns: The equivalent angle in degrees.

name: randomIntegerBetween
description: Pick a random integer in an inclusive range.
returns: A random integer `n` where `min <= n <= max`.

name: randomNumberBetween
description: Pick a random floating-point number in a half-open range.
returns: A random number `n` where `min <= n < max`.

name: createRect
signature: (width: number, height: number, angle?: number, options?: { rotate?: boolean; rotateSpeed?: number; clockwise?: boolean; time?: number; }) => { vertices: Point[]; }
description: Compute the four corner vertices of a rectangle, centered on the origin.

params.name: angle
params.text: Rotation in radians about the center. Defaults to `0`.

params.name: options
params.text:
Optional spin animation: `{ rotate, rotateSpeed, clockwise, time }`. When
`rotate` is true the angle advances with `options.time` (ms, e.g. `performance.now()`),
keeping the function pure — pass the time in rather than reading the clock here.

returns: `{ vertices }` — the four corners as points.

name: rectToPolygon
signature: (rx: number, ry: number, rw: number, rh: number, vertices: Point[]) => boolean
description:
Test whether an axis-aligned rectangle overlaps a polygon.

Combines three checks so no overlap is missed:
1. Any polygon vertex inside the rectangle.
2. Any rectangle corner inside the polygon.
3. Any polygon edge crossing any rectangle edge.

The vertex-only approach used by most simple implementations fails when one
shape is entirely inside the other, or when edges cross without either shape's
vertices being contained. All three checks together are complete.
returns: `true` if the rectangle and polygon overlap.

name: rectToRect
signature: (x1: number, y1: number, w1: number, h1: number, x2: number, y2: number, w2: number, h2: number) => boolean
description: Test whether two axis-aligned rectangles overlap (AABB collision).
returns: `true` if the two rectangles have positive overlapping area.

name: sineCurve
signature: (startingValue: number, differential: number, speed: number, time: number) => number
description:
Oscillate a value smoothly around a baseline using a sine wave.

Pure and deterministic: pass the current `time` (e.g. `performance.now()`) and the
same inputs always produce the same output — so it's easy to test and reproduce.

params.name: startingValue
params.text: The baseline the result oscillates around.

params.name: differential
params.text: The amplitude (how far above/below the baseline it swings).

returns: The oscillating value at the given moment.
example: const y = sineCurve(200, 50, 0.002, performance.now()); // bob around y=200 by ±50

name: sineWave
signature: (x: number, centerY: number, amplitude: number, wavelength: number, phase: number) => number
description:
Sample the height of a sine wave at a horizontal position.

The standard wave equation: `centerY + amplitude · sin(2π·(x/wavelength + phase))`.
Animate by advancing `phase` over time to make the wave travel.

params.name: phase
params.text: Phase offset in wavelengths; advance over time to animate.

returns: The wave's y value at `x`.

name: smootherstep
signature: (edge0: number, edge1: number, x: number) => number
description:
Ken Perlin's smoother variant of {@link smoothstep}.

Uses a 5th-order curve so both the 1st *and* 2nd derivatives are zero at the
edges — even less perceptible "popping" at the start and end of the transition.

params.name: edge0
params.text: Lower edge; output is 0 at or below this.

params.name: edge1
params.text: Upper edge; output is 1 at or above this.

returns: A very smoothly eased value in `[0, 1]`.

name: smoothstep
signature: (edge0: number, edge1: number, x: number) => number
description:
Smooth Hermite interpolation between two edges — the classic GLSL `smoothstep`.

Returns 0 below `edge0`, 1 above `edge1`, and an eased S-curve in between (flat
at both ends), giving gentler transitions than a raw linear ramp.

params.name: edge0
params.text: Lower edge; output is 0 at or below this.

params.name: edge1
params.text: Upper edge; output is 1 at or above this.

returns: A smoothly eased value in `[0, 1]`.

name: sphereLighting
signature: (sphere: { x: number; y: number; radius: number; }, lightSource: Point, highlightReach?: number) => Point
description:
Find where the specular highlight sits on a 2D sphere, given a light source.

Points from the sphere's center toward the light and walks part of the way out
along the radius — a cheap way to fake a 3D-lit highlight on a flat circle.

params.name: highlightReach
params.text: How far toward the edge the highlight sits, 0–1. Defaults to `0.4`.

returns: The `{ x, y }` position to draw the highlight.
example: const hi = sphereLighting({ x: 100, y: 100, radius: 40 }, { x: 0, y: 0 });

name: starVertices
signature: (spikes: number, innerRadius: number, outerRadius: number, angle?: number, options?: { rotate?: boolean; rotateSpeed?: number; clockwise?: boolean; time?: number; }) => { vertices: Point[]; }
description: Compute the vertices of a star polygon, alternating between an outer and inner radius.

params.name: spikes
params.text: Number of points on the star.

params.name: angle
params.text: Starting rotation in radians. Defaults to `0`.

params.name: options
params.text:
Optional spin animation: `{ rotate, rotateSpeed, clockwise, time }`. When
`rotate` is true the angle advances with `options.time` (ms, e.g. `performance.now()`),
keeping the function pure — pass the time in rather than reading the clock here.

returns: `{ vertices }` — `spikes * 2` points, alternating outer then inner.

name: unitCirclePoint
signature: (cx: number, cy: number, radius: number, time: number) => { x: number; y: number; cos: number; sin: number; }
description:
Get the point on a circle at a given angle, along with the raw cosine/sine.

Returning `cos`/`sin` too saves the caller recomputing them (e.g. to orient
something tangent to the circle).

params.name: time
params.text: Angle in radians (named `time` since it's usually driven by a clock).

returns: `{ x, y, cos, sin }` — the point and the trig components used to find it.

name: vecAdd
description: Add two vectors component-wise.
returns: A new vector `{ x: a.x + b.x, y: a.y + b.y }`.

name: vecAngle
description: The angle of a vector in radians, measured counter-clockwise from the positive x-axis.
returns: The angle in radians, in the range `-π`…`π` (via `Math.atan2`).

name: vecAngleBetween
description: The (unsigned) angle between two vectors.
returns: The angle in radians, in the range `0`…`π`. Returns `0` if either vector is zero-length.
example: vecAngleBetween({ x: 1, y: 0 }, { x: 0, y: 1 }); // => Math.PI / 2

name: vecCross
description:
2D cross product — the z-component of the 3D cross, a single scalar.

Its sign tells you the turn direction from `a` to `b` (positive = counter-clockwise),
and its magnitude is the area of the parallelogram they span.
returns: The scalar `a.x * b.y - a.y * b.x`.

name: vecDot
description:
Dot product of two vectors.

A measure of how much the vectors point the same way: positive when roughly
aligned, zero when perpendicular, negative when opposed. The basis for projection.
returns: The scalar `a.x * b.x + a.y * b.y`.

name: vecLerp
signature: (a: Vector, b: Vector, t: number) => Vector
description: Linearly interpolate between two vectors (lerp in 2D).
returns: A new vector partway between `a` and `b`.

name: vecLimit
description:
Clamp a vector's magnitude to at most `max`, preserving its direction.

Common for capping a velocity to a maximum speed.
returns: A new vector no longer than `max` (a copy of `v` if it was already shorter).
example: vecLimit({ x: 30, y: 40 }, 10); // => { x: 6, y: 8 } (was length 50, now length 10)

name: vecMagnitude
description: Length (magnitude) of a vector.
returns: Its length, `√(x² + y²)`.

name: vecMagnitudeSquared
description:
Squared length of a vector — cheaper than {@link vecMagnitude} (skips the square root).

Use this when you only need to *compare* lengths (e.g. "which is closer?"), since
comparing squared lengths gives the same answer without the costly `sqrt`.
returns: `x² + y²`.

name: vecNormalize
description: Normalize a vector to length 1, preserving its direction (a "unit vector").
returns: A new unit vector, or `{ x: 0, y: 0 }` if `v` has zero length.

name: vecPerpendicular
description:
A vector perpendicular to the given one (rotated 90° counter-clockwise).

Handy for finding a surface normal from an edge.
returns: A new vector `{ x: -v.y, y: v.x }`.

name: vecReflect
description: Reflect a vector off a surface, like a ball bouncing off a wall.

params.name: normal
params.text: The surface normal. Should be a unit vector; normalize it first if unsure.

returns: The reflected vector, `v - 2 (v · normal) normal`.
example: vecReflect({ x: 1, y: -1 }, { x: 0, y: 1 }); // => { x: 1, y: 1 } (bounce off the floor)

name: vecRotate
description: Rotate a vector by an angle (counter-clockwise in standard math coordinates).
returns: A new, rotated vector of the same length.
example: vecRotate({ x: 1, y: 0 }, Math.PI / 2); // => { x: ~0, y: 1 }

name: vecScale
description: Scale a vector by a scalar (stretch or shrink it, keeping direction unless negative).
returns: A new vector `{ x: v.x * s, y: v.y * s }`.

name: vecSubtract
description: Subtract vector `b` from `a` — the displacement pointing from `b` to `a`.
returns: A new vector `{ x: a.x - b.x, y: a.y - b.y }`.

name: waveAmplitude
signature: (x: number, y: number, sources: Point[], time: number, k: number, omega: number) => number
description:
Compute the combined wave height at a point from multiple ripple sources (interference).

Sums a traveling cosine wave radiating from each source and averages them, so
overlapping waves reinforce (bright) or cancel (dark) — the two-slit interference
pattern. `k` is the spatial frequency (2π/wavelength), `omega` the temporal frequency.

params.name: time
params.text: Current time, advanced each frame to animate the waves.

returns: The averaged wave amplitude at the point, roughly `-1`…`1`.
example: const a = waveAmplitude(x, y, [s1, s2], time, 0.05, 0.1);

name: wrap
signature: (value: number, min: number, max: number) => number
description:
Wrap a value into the half-open range `[min, max)`, looping around at the ends.

Like a modulo that respects an arbitrary lower bound — values that run off one
end reappear at the other (think Asteroids-style screen wrap, or cyclic indices).
returns: `value` mapped into `[min, max)`. Returns `min` if the range is empty.
